name: Compute Engine Virtual Machines
description: Creating and managing virtual machines on Google Cloud, including machine types, images, disks, startup scripts, and instance groups.
key_facts: Machine types: General-purpose (E2, N2, N4), Compute-optimized (C2, C3), Memory-optimized (M1, M2, M3), GPU (A2, G2)
Preemptible/Spot VMs offer 60-91% discount but can be reclaimed by Google with 30 seconds notice
Persistent disks are network-attached and survive VM deletion; local SSDs are ephemeral and attached to the host
Instance templates define VM configuration and are required for managed instance groups (MIGs)
Startup scripts (metadata key: startup-script) run as root on boot and can install software or configure the VM
common_misconceptions: Stopping a VM stops all billing — stopped VMs still incur charges for attached persistent disks and reserved static IPs
Preemptible VMs are always preempted after 24 hours — they may be preempted sooner but 24 hours is the maximum runtime

name: Managed Instance Groups and Autoscaling
description: Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) provide automated VM lifecycle management with autoscaling, autohealing, and rolling updates for high availability.
key_facts: MIGs maintain a desired number of identical VM instances using an instance template
Autoscaling triggers on CPU utilization, HTTP load balancing serving capacity, Cloud Monitoring metrics, or schedules
Autohealing uses health checks to detect and recreate unhealthy VM instances automatically
Regional MIGs distribute instances across multiple zones for higher availability than zonal MIGs
Rolling updates (proactive or opportunistic) replace instances gradually without downtime using max-surge and max-unavailable settings
common_misconceptions: MIGs automatically load balance traffic — you still need to attach a Cloud Load Balancer; MIGs only manage instance count
Autohealing immediately replaces failed instances — there is an initial delay and health check grace period before replacement

name: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Fundamentals
description: Deploying and managing containerized workloads on GKE, including cluster modes, node pools, workload deployment, and cluster upgrades.
key_facts: GKE Autopilot manages node provisioning, scaling, and security automatically; Standard mode gives node-level control
Node pools are groups of nodes within a cluster that share the same configuration (machine type, labels, taints)
kubectl is the command-line tool for interacting with GKE clusters; gcloud container clusters get-credentials configures kubectl
Workload Identity is the recommended way to grant GKE workloads access to Google Cloud services without service account keys
GKE release channels (Rapid, Regular, Stable) control the cadence of Kubernetes version upgrades
common_misconceptions: GKE Autopilot has no cost when pods are idle — you pay for pod resource requests even in Autopilot mode
GKE and Kubernetes are the same — GKE is a managed Kubernetes service; Kubernetes is the open-source orchestrator

name: Cloud Run Serverless Containers
description: Deploying and managing stateless containerized services on Cloud Run with automatic scaling including scale-to-zero and HTTPS endpoint management.
key_facts: Cloud Run scales from zero to thousands of container instances based on incoming requests automatically
Containers must listen on the port defined by the PORT environment variable (default 8080)
Services (HTTP requests) and Jobs (batch tasks) are the two main Cloud Run resource types
VPC connector allows Cloud Run services to access resources in a VPC, such as Cloud SQL via private IP
Minimum instances setting prevents cold starts by keeping warm instances ready at all times
common_misconceptions: Cloud Run only supports HTTP workloads — Cloud Run Jobs support batch/background processing without HTTP triggers
Scale-to-zero means no cost when idle — you only pay for request processing time; idle instances cost nothing unless minimum instances is set

name: IAM Roles, Service Accounts, and Permissions
description: Configuring granular access control using IAM roles and service accounts to implement least privilege across Google Cloud resources.
key_facts: Basic roles (Owner, Editor, Viewer) are coarse-grained and should be avoided in production; use predefined or custom roles
Service accounts are identities for non-human workloads; key files should be avoided in favor of Workload Identity or attached service accounts
IAM conditions allow attribute-based access control based on resource type, name, time, or request attributes
The gcloud command for granting roles: gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --member=... --role=...
Policy troubleshooting and IAM recommender help identify overly permissive roles and suggest least-privilege alternatives
common_misconceptions: Deleting a service account key immediately revokes its access — there may be a propagation delay of up to 60 seconds
Primitive roles are fine for small teams — they grant broad permissions and violate least privilege regardless of team size

name: Resource Hierarchy and Organization Policies
description: Managing Google Cloud's resource hierarchy (Organization > Folders > Projects > Resources) and using organization policies to enforce guardrails.
key_facts: Resource hierarchy: Organization > Folders > Projects > Resources (VMs, buckets, etc.)
IAM policies and organization policies inherit downward; child resources cannot override parent deny policies
Organization policies enforce constraints across the organization (e.g., restrict resource locations, disable service account key creation)
Projects are the fundamental unit of resource organization, billing, and quota management
Resource Manager lets you move projects between folders and apply labels for cost attribution
common_misconceptions: Project-level IAM policy overrides organization-level policy — inheritance flows downward and more restrictive policies take precedence
Folders are required in a GCP organization — folders are optional; projects can exist directly under the organization

name: VPC Networking and Subnets
description: Designing and configuring Virtual Private Cloud networks, subnets, firewall rules, and routes to connect GCP resources securely.
key_facts: GCP VPCs are global; subnets are regional and have primary and secondary IP ranges
Auto-mode VPC creates subnets in each region automatically; custom-mode gives full control over IP ranges
Firewall rules are stateful and evaluated by priority (0-65534, lower wins) with implied deny-all at the end
VPC peering connects two VPCs without transitive routing; Shared VPC centralizes networking for multiple projects
Cloud Router with BGP advertises routes between VPC and on-premises networks for Cloud VPN and Interconnect
common_misconceptions: VPC peering is transitive — VPC A peered with B and B peered with C does not mean A can reach C
GCP firewall rules work like traditional stateless ACLs — GCP firewalls are stateful, tracking connection state

name: Cloud Load Balancing
description: Selecting and configuring the appropriate Google Cloud load balancer type based on protocol, traffic origin (internal/external), and geographic scope.
key_facts: External Application Load Balancer (HTTP/S) is global, uses Anycast, and supports Cloud CDN and URL maps
External Network Load Balancer (TCP/UDP) is regional and preserves client source IP addresses
Internal Load Balancers route traffic within VPC: Internal Application LB (HTTP/S) and Internal Passthrough NLB (TCP/UDP)
Backend services configure health checks, session affinity, and traffic distribution to instance groups or NEGs
Cloud CDN integrates with External Application Load Balancer to cache content at Google's edge PoPs
common_misconceptions: HTTP(S) Load Balancer terminates SSL at the backend VMs — it terminates SSL at the load balancer level, decrypting at the edge
Load balancers are regional — the External Application LB is global with a single anycast IP, serving users from nearest PoP

name: Cloud Storage Buckets and Access Control
description: Creating and managing Cloud Storage buckets with appropriate storage classes, lifecycle policies, versioning, and access control configurations.
key_facts: Storage classes: Standard (frequent access), Nearline (30-day min, monthly access), Coldline (90-day min), Archive (365-day min)
Lifecycle policies automate object transitions between storage classes or deletion based on age or condition
Object versioning retains overwritten/deleted objects; combined with lifecycle rules to manage version count and cost
Bucket-level access (uniform) vs object-level ACLs (fine-grained); Google recommends uniform bucket-level access
Signed URLs grant time-limited access to private objects without requiring Google authentication
common_misconceptions: Deleting a bucket is reversible — deleted buckets and objects (without versioning) are permanently gone with no recovery
Nearline is for cold archival data — Nearline is for monthly access; use Archive for rarely accessed long-term storage

name: Cloud SQL Managed Relational Databases
description: Deploying and configuring fully managed MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server instances on Cloud SQL with high availability, read replicas, and backups.
key_facts: Cloud SQL supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server with automatic patching and backups
High availability (HA) uses a standby instance in a different zone with synchronous replication and automatic failover
Read replicas offload read traffic and can be in the same region or cross-region for disaster recovery
Private IP access uses VPC peering or Private Service Connect to avoid exposing databases to public internet
Point-in-time recovery (PITR) enables restoring to any second within the backup retention window using binary logs
common_misconceptions: Cloud SQL HA provides zero RPO — a small amount of data loss is possible during failover due to replication lag
Cloud SQL auto-scales storage and compute — storage can autoscale but compute (vCPUs/RAM) requires manual or scheduled changes

name: Cloud Monitoring and Logging
description: Setting up monitoring dashboards, alert policies, log-based metrics, and log exports using Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging.
key_facts: Cloud Logging automatically collects logs from all GCP services; VM logs require the Ops Agent or legacy Logging agent
Log sinks export logs to Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Pub/Sub, or other Google Cloud projects for long-term retention
Log-based metrics create custom metrics from log entries that can trigger alerts in Cloud Monitoring
Uptime checks test the availability of URLs, TCP ports, or DNS from multiple geographic locations
Alert policies define conditions, notification channels, and incident creation rules for proactive issue detection
common_misconceptions: Cloud Logging retains logs forever by default — default log retention is 30 days for most logs; use log sinks for longer retention
Cloud Monitoring only monitors GCP resources — it supports monitoring on-premises and multi-cloud via the Ops Agent

name: Infrastructure as Code with Terraform and Deployment Manager
description: Automating GCP infrastructure provisioning using Terraform (Google-recommended) or Cloud Deployment Manager templates.
key_facts: Terraform is Google's recommended IaC tool with a Google Cloud provider and state management via Cloud Storage
Cloud Deployment Manager uses YAML/Jinja2 templates and is GCP-native but less feature-rich than Terraform
IaC enables repeatable, version-controlled infrastructure; changes are previewed with terraform plan before applying
Terraform state tracks current infrastructure; remote state in Cloud Storage enables team collaboration
Cloud Foundation Toolkit provides opinionated Terraform modules aligned with Google's best practices
common_misconceptions: Deployment Manager and Terraform achieve identical results — Terraform supports multi-cloud and has a much richer ecosystem
IaC eliminates all manual configuration — IaC manages provisioning but operational config (OS-level) still needs configuration management tools

name: GCP Billing, Quotas, and Cost Management
description: Configuring billing accounts, budgets, alerts, and cost optimization strategies including committed use discounts and sustained use discounts.
key_facts: Billing accounts are linked to projects; one billing account can pay for multiple projects
Sustained Use Discounts (SUD) apply automatically when VMs run more than 25% of a month with no commitment required
Committed Use Discounts (CUD) offer 1 or 3-year commitments for up to 57% discount on vCPUs and memory
Budget alerts send notifications when spending reaches configured thresholds (50%, 90%, 100% of budget)
Labels applied to resources enable cost attribution by team, environment, or project in billing exports
common_misconceptions: Sustained use discounts require enrollment — they apply automatically based on usage with no sign-up needed
Budget alerts stop spending when the budget is exceeded — they only send notifications; you must implement programmatic responses

name: App Engine Standard and Flexible Environments
description: Deploying web applications on App Engine Standard (language-specific runtimes) vs Flexible (Docker containers) with automatic scaling and traffic splitting.
key_facts: Standard environment uses sandboxed language runtimes (Python, Java, Go, Node.js) and can scale to zero
Flexible environment runs Docker containers on Compute Engine VMs and supports any language or runtime
Traffic splitting routes percentages of traffic to different versions for A/B testing or canary deployments
Services (formerly modules) allow a single App Engine app to be composed of multiple independently scaled services
app.yaml defines runtime, scaling configuration, environment variables, and URL routing for each service
common_misconceptions: App Engine Flexible is always more capable than Standard — Standard has faster scaling and can reach zero instances; Flexible has minimum 1 instance
You can use any library in App Engine Standard — Standard uses a sandboxed runtime with restrictions; Flexible has no such limitations

name: gcloud CLI and Google Cloud SDK
description: Using the gcloud command-line tool for GCP resource management, including configuration, authentication, and key commands tested on the ACE exam.
key_facts: gcloud init configures the SDK with account, project, and region/zone defaults for a new installation
gcloud config set and gcloud config configurations manage multiple project/account contexts
Key patterns: gcloud compute instances create, gcloud container clusters create, gcloud storage cp
gcloud auth application-default login sets credentials for application-level authentication
Cloud Shell provides a browser-based terminal with gcloud pre-installed and 5 GB persistent home directory
common_misconceptions: gcloud and gsutil are different tools requiring separate installation — gsutil is included in the Cloud SDK alongside gcloud
Cloud Shell is a permanent VM — Cloud Shell instances are ephemeral; only the home directory persists across sessions
